--- Page 1 ---
Interpretable Clustering via Optimal
Multiway-split Decision Trees
Hayato Suzuki1, Shunnosuke Ikeda2, and
Yuichi Takano3
1 Graduate School of Science and Technology, University of Tsukuba,
1–1–1 Tennodai, Tsukuba-shi, 305–8573, Ibaraki, Japan
s2420503@u.tsukuba.ac.jp
2 Institute of Systems and Information Engineering, University of Tsukuba,
1–1–1 Tennodai, Tsukuba-shi, 305–8573, Ibaraki, Japan
ikeda@cs.tsukuba.ac.jp
3 Institute of Systems and Information Engineering, University of Tsukuba,
1–1–1 Tennodai, Tsukuba-shi, 305–8573, Ibaraki, Japan
ytakano@sk.tsukuba.ac.jp
Abstract. Clustering serves as a vital tool for uncovering latent data
structures, and achieving both high accuracy and interpretability is es-
sential. To this end, existing methods typically construct binary deci-
sion trees by solving mixed-integer nonlinear optimization problems, of-
ten leading to significant computational costs and suboptimal solutions.
Furthermore, binary decision trees frequently result in excessively deep
structures, which makes them difficult to interpret. To mitigate these
issues, we propose an interpretable clustering method based on optimal
multiway-split decision trees, formulated as a 0–1 integer linear optimiza-
tion problem. This reformulation renders the optimization problem more
tractable compared to existing models. A key feature of our method is the
integration of a one-dimensional K-means algorithm for the discretiza-
tion of continuous variables, allowing for flexible and data-driven branch-
ing. Extensive numerical experiments on publicly available real-world
datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms baseline methods
in terms of clustering accuracy and interpretability. Our method yields
multiway-split decision trees with concise decision rules while maintain-
ing competitive performance across various evaluation metrics.
Keywords: Interpretable clustering · Optimal multiway-split desicion
trees · 0–1 integer linear optimization · Feature discretization
1
Introduction
1.1
Background
Clustering serves as a fundamental unsupervised learning technique that orga-
nizes data points into groups exhibiting similar characteristics. Its primary ob-
jective involves uncovering latent data structures to facilitate decision-making
arXiv:2602.13586v1  [cs.LG]  14 Feb 2026


--- Page 2 ---
2
H. Suzuki et al.
and enhance the performance of predictive models. The output of this process
typically forms a partition of a dataset. Clustering accuracy reflects the extent
to which these clusters represent the underlying structure and is critical because
it directly influences data analysis quality. Additionally, it impacts the efficacy
of downstream predictive models when used as a preprocessing step.
However, accuracy is not the sole determinant of utility in practical applica-
tions. The interpretability of the results often dictates their value for planning
actions and knowledge discovery. This concept refers to the capacity to compre-
hend the characteristics of each cluster and explain the resulting structure [11].
For instance, stratifying patients with similar features in healthcare settings
helps identify population structures regarding age or diseases and supports clin-
ical decision-making [3].
Despite the recognized significance of cluster interpretability, research di-
rectly addressing this challenge remains limited [16]. Consequently, the devel-
opment of methods that simultaneously achieve high clustering accuracy and
interpretability has emerged as a vital research endeavor.
1.2
Related work
A wide variety of clustering methods have been proposed to date. K-means [22] is
a representative partitioning method that divides data into a pre-specified num-
ber of clusters. Although it is sensitive to initialization, recent advancements in
initialization strategies [1] have significantly enhanced its stability and accuracy.
Hierarchical clustering [26] constructs a nested structure of clusters based on
a defined distance metric. Model-based clustering [15] represents clusters using
mixture models of probability distributions and captures flexible data-generating
processes by estimating parameters including means and covariances. In contrast,
density-based clustering [10] identifies high-density regions as clusters and proves
effective for detecting arbitrarily shaped clusters and outliers.
The need for machine learning methods that are both accurate and inter-
pretable has also been emphasized by regulatory bodies such as the European
Union [13]. The use of representative points [8] to interpret clusters is a widely
adopted practice, but it may fail to capture essential characteristics when clusters
are elongated or exhibit anisotropic distributions [18]. To address this issue, one
may additionally compute summary statistics such as per-dimension variances,
but doing so increases the amount of information to be interpreted and thus
raises the interpretive burden. Another common strategy is to visualize data by
projecting it onto two dimensions using principal component analysis [19], but
this can obscure the correspondence between clusters and the original features.
In this regard, decision-tree-based methods are highly interpretable because
they express cluster characteristics explicitly through splitting rules. As a simple
way to apply decision-tree-based methods to unsupervised learning, a two-stage
hybrid scheme first performs clustering and then explains the resulting clusters
using a decision tree. Specifically, partitioning or hierarchical clustering is applied
first, and then trains a decision tree using the obtained assignments as class
labels to describe the cluster structure [18]. Hancock et al. [14] used decision


--- Page 3 ---
Interpretable Clustering via Optimal Multiway-split Decision Trees
3
trees to interpret and refine the results of hierarchical clustering on sea surface
temperature data. However, because cluster formation and rule construction
are performed under different frameworks, the consistency between them is not
guaranteed. Consequently, the learned splits may fail to faithfully reflect the
actual cluster structure.
To overcome this limitation, several algorithms have been proposed that in-
corporate interpretability directly into the cluster formation stage. Chavent et
al. [7] developed a binary-tree-based clustering algorithm after transforming the
feature space into one in which clusters are easier to separate, and Basak and Kr-
ishnapuram [2] proposed an extension that incorporates feature selection. How-
ever, because these methods transform the feature space, the interpretability of
the resulting clusters can be diminished. In contrast, other studies build decision
trees directly in the original feature space. Liu et al. [21] converted the clustering
problem into a binary classification task and modified the split purity criterion
to simultaneously identify dense and sparse regions, but their method requires
a preprocessing step that adds synthetic data.
Blockeel et al. [4] proposed a general framework called the Predictive Cluster-
ing Tree (PCT) and presented a tree-construction algorithm applicable to both
supervised and unsupervised learning. Furthermore, Fraiman et al. [12] proposed
Clustering Using Unsupervised Binary Trees (CUBT) and developed a greedy
algorithm that iteratively selects a feature and threshold to split the data into
two parts so as to reduce the within-cluster sum of squares. While these meth-
ods represent progress toward directly constructing clusters via tree structures,
they are all based on local greedy search and thus are limited in their ability
to flexibly incorporate different cluster evaluation criteria. In the survey by Hu
et al. [16], definitions of interpretability in clustering and methods to achieve it
are systematically reviewed, and optimization-based methods are highlighted as
particularly promising.
Building on these lines of work, Bertsimas et al. [3] extended optimal classifi-
cation trees (OCT) to unsupervised learning and proposed interpretable cluster-
ing via optimal trees (ICOT), a method that simultaneously optimizes clustering
and tree construction using mixed-integer optimization. In ICOT, each leaf node
of the tree corresponds to a cluster. This structure provides high interpretability
by explicitly representing splitting rules while improving clustering accuracy. An-
other key feature is that ICOT incorporates within-cluster cohesion and between-
cluster separation into its objective, allowing diverse evaluation metrics such as
the silhouette coefficient and the Dunn index to be flexibly adopted. However,
ICOT is formulated as a mixed-integer nonlinear optimization problem, which
incurs a high computational cost, and its restriction to binary decision trees
tends to produce deeper trees, thereby reducing interpretability.
In contrast, Subramanian et al. [28] proposed the Optimal Multiway-split
Decision Tree (OMT), which optimizes a multiway-split tree structure for su-
pervised learning. OMT introduces a feature graph, a layered directed graph in
which each layer corresponds to a feature and each node in a layer represents a
discretized value (bin) of that feature. For continuous features, the value range


--- Page 4 ---
4
H. Suzuki et al.
is first discretized into a finite set of intervals, and each interval is treated as a
node. For categorical features, each category is treated as a node. In addition, a
Skip node is included in each feature layer to represent that the corresponding
feature is not used in a rule. Directed edges connect every node in one layer to
every node in the next layer, and a Source node and a Sink node are added at
the beginning and end of the graph, respectively. A directed path from Source
to Sink therefore represents a conjunction of feature conditions, i.e., a com-
bination of discretized feature values (possibly skipping some features). OMT
enumerates such paths as candidate decision rules and constructs a multiway-
split decision tree by selecting an optimal subset of paths via mixed-integer
optimization. By adopting a multiway-split decision tree structure, OMT yields
more concise splitting rules than binary decision trees and has been reported to
maintain high interpretability with shallower trees. However, OMT is designed
for supervised learning and cannot be directly applied to clustering, which is an
unsupervised learning task.
Thus, ICOT supports unsupervised learning but is restricted to binary deci-
sion trees, whereas OMT can handle multiway-split decision tree structures but
is limited to supervised learning. This paper integrates the strengths of these two
methods and proposes a new clustering method that leverages multiway decision
tree structures within an unsupervised learning framework to achieve both high
accuracy and interpretability.
1.3
Our contribution
The goal of this paper is to integrate existing methods, ICOT [3] and OMT [28],
and to design and evaluate an interpretable clustering method based on optimal
multiway-split decision trees.
The main contributions of this study are summarized as follows.
1. Formulation of clustering with multiway-split tree structures
We extend the OMT architecture to the unsupervised learning domain and
formulate the clustering problem as a 0–1 integer linear optimization prob-
lem. This provides an optimization framework for interpretable clustering
based on multiway-split tree structures.
2. Adaptive discretization for continuous variables
Instead of the cumulative binning used in OMT, we adopt one-dimensional
K-means based on the data distribution. This enables appropriate discretiza-
tion of continuous variables and improves the expressiveness and accuracy
of cluster boundaries.
3. Performance validation on public datasets
Through numerical experiments on multiple public datasets, we confirm
that the proposed method outperforms existing methods (ICOT [3], CART-
Hybrid [23], and PCT [4]) in terms of both accuracy and interpretability.


--- Page 5 ---
Interpretable Clustering via Optimal Multiway-split Decision Trees
5
2
Clustering via optimal multiway-split decision trees
In this section, we extend the framework of OMT to unsupervised learning and
propose an interpretable clustering method via optimal optimal multi-split de-
cision trees (ICOMT).
2.1
Overview
To construct a multiway-split decision tree, we first discretize continuous features
into a finite set of intervals and treat each interval as a node (and each category
for categorical features likewise). We then build a “feature graph” by representing
these discretized values as nodes and connecting nodes across different features
with directed edges. Fig. 1 illustrates an example with two features (gender and
age). A directed path from Source to Sink corresponds to a combination of
discretized feature values. Here, a Skip node indicates that the corresponding
feature is not used as a splitting condition. Directed edges are created for all
combinations of feature values, and each path on the graph corresponds to a
specific sequence of feature conditions (e.g., x1 = male, x2 = under 20).
By enumerating all paths from Source to Sink on the feature graph and
selecting an optimal subset of paths, we can construct a multiway-split decision
tree structure. For example, if the red path in Fig. 1 is selected, the corresponding
multiway-split decision tree can be represented as in Fig. 2. In this setting, each
path corresponds to a leaf node (cluster) of the tree, and the set of data points
that satisfy the feature conditions along the path from the root is defined as a
cluster. That is, because each path in the multiway-split tree directly represents
a cluster’s splitting rule, the entire model can be visualized as an explicit logical
structure, yielding high interpretability.
In this way, by viewing the construction of a multiway-split decision tree as
a path selection problem on a feature graph, OMT can optimize the splitting
structure of the entire tree.
Fig. 1. An example of a feature graph


--- Page 6 ---
6
H. Suzuki et al.
Fig. 2. A multiway-split decision tree obtained from the red path in Fig. 1
2.2
Formulation
In this section, we formulate clustering based on OMT as a 0–1 integer linear
optimization problem. In our setting, each path on the feature graph corresponds
to a candidate cluster, and the goal is to select an optimal set of paths from these
candidates.
The notation used in this formulation is summarized in Table 1.
Table 1. Notation used in our formulation
Category
Symbol
Description
Decision variables zj ∈{0, 1}
1 if path j is selected
Index sets
i ∈I = {1, 2, . . . , N} Set of data points
j ∈J
Set of paths
Constants
ξj
Error of path j (within-cluster sum of squares)
aij ∈{0, 1}
1 if data point i is covered by path j
nj
Number of data points covered by path j
l
Maximum number of leaves (clusters)
ρ ∈[0, 1]
Coverage ratio
N
Number of data points
Let xi be a p-dimensional feature vector, and define the dataset as follows.
D = {xi ∈Rp | i ∈I}
The within-cluster sum of squares ξj associated with path j is computed
using the set of data points covered by path j, Cj = {i ∈I | aij = 1}, and its
centroid (cluster mean) µj.
ξj =
X
i∈Cj
∥xi −µj∥2,
µj =
1
|Cj|
X
i∈Cj
xi
(1)


--- Page 7 ---
Interpretable Clustering via Optimal Multiway-split Decision Trees
7
This quantity is a constant that can be precomputed once the feature graph and
the candidate path set are fixed.
Using the above definitions, let z = (zj)j∈J ∈{0, 1}|J| denote the binary
path-selection vector, where zj = 1 if and only if path j is selected. The cluster-
ing problem is then formulated as the following 0–1 integer linear optimization
problem that selects an optimal set of paths:
min
z
X
j∈J
ξjzj
(2)
s. t.
X
j∈J
aijzj ≤1,
∀i ∈I,
(3)
X
j∈J
zj ≤l,
(4)
X
j∈J
njzj ≥ρN,
(5)
zj ∈{0, 1},
∀j ∈J.
(6)
The objective function and constraints are as follows:
– Objective function (2): Minimize the total within-cluster sum of squares ξj
associated with the selected paths j. This encourages data points within each
cluster to be compact, thereby increasing cluster cohesion.
– Constraint (3): Each data point i is assigned to at most one path (cluster).
– Constraint (4): The number of selected paths (clusters) is at most l. This
imposes an upper bound on the number of clusters.
– Constraint (5): The selected paths must cover at least ρN of all data points,
where ρ is a parameter specifying the minimum coverage ratio, which helps
prevent selecting paths that mainly capture anomalous points.
– Constraint (6): zj is a binary variable indicating whether path j is selected.
2.3
Discretization of continuous variables
In ICOMT and OMT, when features take continuous values, it is necessary to
partition their ranges into discrete intervals in order to define splitting bound-
aries. That is, as a preprocessing step, we discretize continuous features by con-
verting them into a finite number of intervals.
Let f ∈[p] := {1, 2, . . . , p} index the features. In OMT, continuous variables
are discretized using cumulative binning, which consists of “initial binning by
tercile splits” and “merging adjacent intervals”
1. Initial binning by tercile splits
Each continuous variable xf is divided into three intervals so that the number
of data points in each interval is approximately equal. Using the quantiles
Q1 and Q2, the intervals are defined as follows:
(−∞, Q1),
[Q1, Q2),
[Q2, ∞)


--- Page 8 ---
8
H. Suzuki et al.
2. Merging adjacent intervals
Adjacent intervals are merged to generate wider candidate splits.
(−∞, Q2),
[Q1, ∞)
This initial tercile-based binning is effective in maintaining a roughly equal
number of data points in each interval, but it has the drawback that it does
not account for the shape of the data distribution. As a result, when the dis-
tribution is skewed or multimodal, the resulting cluster boundaries may fail to
accurately reflect the underlying data structure, potentially degrading clustering
performance.
To address this issue, we introduce one-dimensional K-means in place of
the initial tercile-based binning. In one-dimensional K-means, each continuous
variable xf is first partitioned into Kf clusters. For a positive integer n, we
use the shorthand notation [n] := {1, 2, . . . , n}. Let xi,f denote the value of the
f-th continuous feature for data point i. The centroid of the k-th cluster for
xf, denoted by cf,k, is computed using the set of data points assigned to that
cluster, Cf,k, and its cardinality |Cf,k|, as follows.
cf,k =
1
|Cf,k|
X
i∈Cf,k
xi,f,
∀f ∈[p],
∀k ∈[Kf].
We then define the midpoint between adjacent centroids, cf,k and cf,k+1, as a
splitting boundary bf,k.
bf,k = cf,k + cf,k+1
2
,
∀f ∈[p], ∀k ∈[Kf −1].
The number of clusters Kf varies for each feature, with candidates Kf ∈{2, 3, . . . , 6}.
The value of Kf that minimizes the BIC is selected.
BIC is a criterion that evaluates the trade-off between goodness of fit (log-
likelihood) and model complexity (degrees of freedom). Using the number of
data points n, the within-cluster sum of squares SSE, and the model degrees of
freedom k (i.e., the number of parameters depending on the number of clusters),
BIC is defined as follows.
BIC = n log(SSE/n) + k log(n)
A smaller BIC indicates a better balance between fit to the data and model
simplicity.
By using discretization based on one-dimensional K-means, we can flexibly
define intervals according to the data distribution and automatically generate
splitting boundaries that are well suited to the cluster structure. As a result,
even for data that include continuous features, it becomes possible to achieve
both interpretable splitting conditions and strong clustering performance.


--- Page 9 ---
Interpretable Clustering via Optimal Multiway-split Decision Trees
9
2.4
Algorithm
In our ICOMT method, after discretizing continuous variables using one-dimensional
K-means, all paths enumerated on the feature graph are treated as candidate
clusters. An optimal set of paths is then selected via 0–1 integer linear opti-
mization to construct a multiway-split tree structure. This section describes the
overall ICOMT algorithm and outlines each step.
The overall procedure of the algorithm is as follows.
1. Discretization of continuous features via initial binning using one-
dimensional K-means
For each continuous feature, we apply one-dimensional K-means with can-
didate numbers of clusters Kf ∈{2, 3, . . . , 6}. For each clustering result, we
compute the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) and select the value of
Kf that minimizes BIC. We define intervals using the midpoints between
the resulting cluster centroids as thresholds, thereby discretizing each fea-
ture. In addition, we merge adjacent intervals based on this initial binning
to represent more flexible splitting boundaries. Through this process, we
achieve adaptive discretization for each feature by accounting for the data
distribution.
2. Construction of the feature graph
We represent each bin of each discretized feature as a node and construct
a feature graph by connecting nodes across different features with directed
edges. In this graph, a path from Source to Sink represents a combination
of feature conditions (e.g., x1 ∈[0.1, 0.3] ∧x2 ∈[0.5, 0.8]).
3. Path enumeration
Using depth-first search on the feature graph, we enumerate all paths from
Source to Sink. Each path is defined as a candidate cluster, and the corre-
sponding set of covered data points is recorded.
4. Redundancy removal and path evaluation
We remove redundant paths from the path set based on the following criteria:
(i) paths that cover the same set of data points, and (ii) paths for which
removing one condition does not change the covered set of data points. For
example, consider two candidate paths j1 : (x1 ∈[0, 0.3]) ∧(x2 ∈[0.5, 0.8])
and j2 : (x1 ∈[0, 0.3])∧(x2 ∈[0.5, 0.8])∧(x3 ∈[0.2, 0.9]). If the set of covered
data points is identical, i.e., {i | aij1 = 1} = {i | aij2 = 1}, then we keep only
one of them by criterion (i). Moreover, if removing the last condition from j2
does not change its covered set, i.e., the condition on x3 is redundant, then
j2 is removed by criterion (ii). This procedure retains a minimal necessary
set of paths and improves the computational efficiency of the optimization.
We then compute the within-cluster sum of squares for each remaining path.
5. Path selection via 0–1 integer linear optimization
For the reduced set of paths, we solve the 0–1 integer linear optimization
problem (2)–(6) to obtain an optimal set of paths that minimizes the total
within-cluster sum of squares.


--- Page 10 ---
10
H. Suzuki et al.
6. Reconstruction of the multiway-split decision tree and cluster as-
signment
We reconstruct the selected set of paths as a multiway-split tree structure,
associating each path with a leaf node. We then assign cluster labels to data
points that satisfy the conditions of each path, thereby explicitly representing
the splitting rules for each cluster. This yields clustering results that balance
accuracy and interpretability.
With this algorithm, even for high-dimensional data that include continuous
values, we can achieve interpretable clustering via a multiway-split tree structure
by combining adaptive discretization with optimal path selection.
3
Numerical experiments
In this section, we evaluate the effectiveness of our method in terms of accuracy
and interpretability by comparing it with existing methods through numerical
experiments on public datasets.
3.1
Datasets
We used the following four datasets. The features used for each dataset are
summarized in Tables 2–5.
3.1.1
Seeds This dataset [6] contains seven geometric features measured from
three varieties of wheat kernels and is widely used as a benchmark for classifi-
cation and clustering. It consists of 210 instances, and for clustering evaluation
we used the three wheat varieties (the target variable) as ground-truth labels.
Table 2. Features used in the Seeds dataset
Feature name
Description
varieties_of_wheat
wheat variety (target variable)
area
area
perimeter
perimeter
compactness
compactness
length_of_kernel
kernel length
width_of_kernel
kernel width
asymmetry_coefficient
asymmetry coefficient
length_of_kernel_groove kernel groove length


--- Page 11 ---
Interpretable Clustering via Optimal Multiway-split Decision Trees
11
3.1.2
Statlog (Vehicle silhouettes) This dataset [25] is designed to dis-
tinguish four types of vehicles based on shape features extracted from vehicle
silhouette images. It contains 845 instances, and for clustering evaluation we used
the four vehicle types (the target variable) as ground-truth labels. In Table 3,
we assigned IDs (Y, X1, X2, . . . , X18) to the target and explanatory variables.
These IDs were used to reference features in the interpretability analysis (Section
3.4.2).
Table 3. Features used in the Statlog (Vehicle Silhouettes) dataset
ID
Feature name
Description
Y
class
Vehicle type (target label)
X1
COMPACTNESS
Degree of compactness of the silhouette
X2
CIRCULARITY
Degree of circularity
X3
DISTANCE CIRCULARITY
Circularity measure based on distances
X4
RADIUS RATIO
Ratio of radii (shape dispersion)
X5
PR.AXIS ASPECT RATIO
Aspect ratio along the principal axis
X6

Aspect ratio based on maximum length
X7
SCATTER RATIO
Ratio capturing the spread/scatter of
the shape
X8

length

X13 SCALED RADIUS OF GYRATION

spread)

X15 SKEWNESS ABOUT MINOR AXIS

X17 KURTOSIS ABOUT MAJOR AXIS

Ratio related to hollowness/concavity
3.1.3
Real Estate Valuation This dataset [30] consists of real estate trans-
action records from the Sindian District, New Taipei City, Taiwan. It is a re-
gression dataset for predicting house price per unit area using six explanatory
variables, including house age, distance to the nearest station, the number of
convenience stores, and latitude and longitude. It contains 414 instances. For
clustering evaluation, we interpreted the target variable (house price of unit
area, Fig. 3) as low, medium, and high price ranges by applying one-dimensional


--- Page 12 ---
12
H. Suzuki et al.
K-means with K = 3. Using the resulting cluster boundaries (32 and 49), we
created three clusters (Fig. 4) and used these labels as ground truth.

3.1.4
Customer purchasing behaviors This dataset [20] is a customer
demographics and purchasing behavior dataset published on Kaggle4, and is
used for tasks such as customer segmentation and purchasing behavior analysis.
It contains 238 instances. For clustering evaluation, we use labels obtained
by truncating the loyalty score (Fig. 5) to an integer value (Fig. 6) as ground
truth.

These four datasets range from low-dimensional datasets with relatively sim-
ple structure to high-dimensional datasets with nonlinear structure. Accordingly,
they enable a multifaceted evaluation of the generality of the ICOMT method
and the effectiveness of the discretization approach.
3.2
Experimental setup
In this section, we describe the baseline methods and experimental settings used
to evaluate the performance of the ICOMT method. We evaluate the methods
from two perspectives: clustering quality and model interpretability.
3.2.1
Methods for comparison To evaluate the effectiveness of our method,
we focused on differences in the multiway-tree structure and discretization strate-
gies for continuous variables, and compared the following five methods.
– ICOMT-K (Proposed): A model that uses an optimal multiway-tree struc-
ture and discretizes continuous variables using one-dimensional K-means.
This is the final version of the proposed method and achieves adaptive,
distribution-based discretization.
– ICOMT-B (Proposed): A model that uses an optimal multiway-tree struc-
ture and discretizes continuous variables using the conventional tercile-based
discretization. This method is used as a comparative baseline to examine the
effect of the discretization strategy.
– ICOT [3]: An interpretable clustering method based on an optimal binary-
tree structure obtained via mixed-integer optimization. It is a representative
existing method proposed for constructing optimal binary decision trees in
unsupervised learning.


--- Page 15 ---
Interpretable Clustering via Optimal Multiway-split Decision Trees
15
– CART-Hybrid [23]: A two-stage hybrid approach that first generates clus-
ter labels using K-means [22] and then trains CART [5] using those labels
as supervision. This is a representative approach that separates clustering
from decision-tree construction.
– PCT [4]: A binary-tree clustering method that sequentially splits clusters
based on a variance-minimization criterion. It is positioned as a greedy ap-
proach to unsupervised tree-structured learning.
The algorithms for the ICOMT-K and ICOMT-B methods followed the pro-
cedure described in Section 2.4, and we used Gurobi Optimizer 12.0.2 5 to solve
the resulting optimization problems. For the ICOT method, we used the heuris-
tic approach proposed in [3], which takes the decision tree constructed by the
CART-Hybrid method as an initial solution. For the PCT method, we trained
a regression tree by treating the input feature vectors themselves as the target
variables so as to minimize variance.
For the experiments, we used a MacBook Pro equipped with an Apple M3
Pro (11 cores) and 18 GB of memory. By comparing the ICOMT method with ex-
isting methods (ICOT, CART-Hybrid, and PCT), we evaluated the effectiveness
of the multiway-tree structure, and by comparing the ICOMT-K and ICOMT-B
methods, we assessed the effect of discretization using one-dimensional K-means.
3.2.2
Parameter settings For all methods, we applied 0–1 normalization to
the input data in advance. In 0–1 normalization, each component xf of a feature
vector xi,f was transformed using its minimum and maximum values as
xnorm
i,f
=
xi,f −min(xf)
max(xf) −min(xf)
so that all features were scaled to the same range [0, 1]. This preprocessing
prevented differences in scale across features from affecting the split criteria, and
it avoided biasing the search for split points and the clustering evaluation toward
specific features. In addition, for all methods, the main parameters related to the
tree structure were set as follows: the maximum depth was 3, and the coverage
rate was set to ρ = 1 so that all data points were included in clustering. We
conducted experiments with the maximum number of clusters ranging from 2 to
10, and selected the number of clusters that maximized the silhouette coefficient
and the Dunn index, which are described later as evaluation metrics.
3.3
Evaluation metrics
To evaluate clustering performance, we used the silhouette coefficient [24] and
the Dunn index [9] as internal evaluation metrics, and the adjusted Rand index
(ARI) [17] as an external evaluation metric.
5 https://www.gurobi.com


--- Page 16 ---
16
H. Suzuki et al.
3.3.1
Silhouette coefficient The silhouette coefficient is an internal met-
ric that comprehensively evaluates within-cluster cohesion and between-cluster
separation. Let the distance between data points i and j be defined as
d(i, j) = ∥xi −xj∥
Let Ci denote the cluster to which data point i belongs. Then, we define the
average distance from i to other data points in the same cluster as
a(i) =
1
|Ci| −1
X
j∈Ci
j̸=i
d(i, j)
and the average distance from i to the nearest different cluster (the neighboring
cluster) as
b(i) = min
C̸=Ci
1
|C|
X
j∈C
d(i, j)
In this case, the silhouette value s(i) for data point i is given by
s(i) =
b(i) −a(i)
max{a(i), b(i)}
If the size of the cluster to which data point i belongs is 1, we define s(i) = 0.
By taking the average over all data points, we obtain the overall silhouette
coefficient S:
S = 1
N
N
X
i=1
s(i)
where N is the total number of data points. The value of S lies in the range
−1 ≤S ≤1. A value closer to 1 indicates a clear cluster structure, 0 indicates
overlapping clusters, and negative values indicate many misassigned points. This
metric is widely used because it can evaluate the balance between within-cluster
cohesion and between-cluster separation.
3.3.2
Dunn index The Dunn index is an internal evaluation metric defined
as the ratio of the minimum inter-cluster distance (separation) to the maximum
intra-cluster distance (compactness). Let the distance between data points be
defined as
d(x, y) = ∥x −y∥.
We define the minimum distance between data points in clusters Ci and Cj as
δ(Ci, Cj) =
min
x∈Ci, y∈Cj d(x, y)
and the maximum distance (diameter) between data points within cluster Ck as
∆(Ck) = max
x,y∈Ck d(x, y).


--- Page 17 ---
Interpretable Clustering via Optimal Multiway-split Decision Trees
17
Let m denote the number of clusters. Then, the Dunn index DI is given by
DI =
min
1≤i<j≤m δ(Ci, Cj)
max
1≤k≤m ∆(Ck)
.
The Dunn index generally takes positive values, and a larger value indicates
clearer separation between clusters and higher internal consistency within clus-
ters. In contrast, a value close to 0 implies that clusters overlap. This metric is
characterized by its emphasis on evaluating separation.
3.3.3
Adjusted Rand index The ARI is an external metric that, when
clustering results and true labels are available, evaluates the agreement between
the two partitions while removing the effect of agreement by chance.
First, we define the Rand index (RI) as follows:
RI =
a + d
a + b + c + d,
where
– a: the number of pairs of data points (i, i′) such that i and i′ are assigned to
the same cluster in the clustering result and also share the same true label
– b: the number of pairs of data points (i, i′) such that i and i′ are assigned to
the same cluster in the clustering result but have different true labels
– c: the number of pairs of data points (i, i′) such that i and i′ are assigned to
different clusters in the clustering result but share the same true label
– d: the number of pairs of data points (i, i′) such that i and i′ are assigned to
different clusters in the clustering result and also have different true labels
Note that a + b + c + d =
␀n
2

, where n is the number of data points.
Because the Rand index is affected by agreement due to chance, the ARI is
defined as a metric that corrects RI by its expected value, as follows:
ARI = RI −E[RI]
1 −E[RI] ,
where E[RI] denotes the expected value of RI under a random assignment of
clusters, i.e., the probability of chance agreement.
In addition, ARI can also be expressed using the contingency table (nij)
between cluster i in the clustering result and true label j as follows:
ARI =
X
ij
nij
2

−
 X
i
ai
2
 X
j
bj
2

/
n
2

1
2
 X
i
ai
2

+
X
j
bj
2

−
 X
i
ai
2
 X
j
bj
2

/
n
2
,


--- Page 18 ---
18
H. Suzuki et al.
where nij denotes the number of data points that belong to both cluster i in the
clustering result and true label j. Moreover, ai = P
j nij is the number of data
points assigned to cluster i, bj = P
i nij is the number of data points with label
j, and n = P
ij nij is the total number of data points.
ARI takes values in the range −1 ≤ARI ≤1, and values closer to 1 indicate
that the cluster structure agrees well with the true labels. A value of 0 means
“agreement at the level of chance,” and negative values indicate “worse-than-
chance agreement”. This metric is one of the most commonly used evaluation
measures when external labels are available. [29,27]
3.4
Experimental Results
3.4.1
Results on Clustering Quality Tables 6–9 report, for each dataset,
the ARI, tree depth, number of clusters, and the total computation time (sec-
onds) required for model construction including preprocessing for each cluster
setting. In each table, we compare ARI, tree depth, number of clusters, and
computation time under the settings that maximize the silhouette coefficient
and those that maximize the Dunn index.
Table 6. Performance of each method on the Seeds dataset

0.004
As shown in Tables 6–9, the ICOMT-K method achieved higher ARI than the
existing methods (ICOT, CART-Hybrid, and PCT) across all datasets. In partic-
ular, on the Seeds and Customer Purchasing Behaviors datasets, the ICOMT-K
method substantially outperformed the existing methods. These results con-
firmed that the ICOMT-K method provided higher clustering accuracy than the
baseline methods.


--- Page 19 ---
Interpretable Clustering via Optimal Multiway-split Decision Trees
19
Table 8. Performance of each method on the Real Estate Valuation dataset
Best Silhouette Coefficient
Best Dunn Index

ARI Tree depth # clusters Computation time (s) ARI Tree depth # clusters Computation time (s)
ICOMT-K

0.146

0.096

0.258

Table 9. Performance of each method on the Customer Purchasing Behaviors dataset
Best Silhouette Coefficient
Best Dunn Index
Method
ARI Tree depth # clusters Computation time (s) ARI Tree depth # clusters Computation time (s)
ICOMT-K
0.595

8
0.0138

3
8
0.0138
ICOMT-B
0.235
1

0.0082
0.452
1
3
0.0072
ICOT
0.550
3
5
0.0679
0.550
3
5
0.0704
CART-Hybrid 0.502
3
6
0.0423
0.502
3
6
0.0362
PCT
0.551
3
5
0.0003
0.551
3
8
0.0003
Furthermore, focusing on tree depth and the number of clusters, we find that
the ICOMT-K method maintains a high ARI while keeping the tree structure rel-
atively simple. On the Seeds dataset (Table 6), the ICOMT-K method achieved
the best ARI with a very shallow multiway-split decision tree of depth 1 and 3
clusters, whereas the ICOT and CART-Hybrid methods required binary deci-
sion trees of depth 3. On the Statlog dataset (Table 7), although the maximum
depth was 3 for all methods, the CART-Hybrid method used as many as 8 clus-
ters, whereas the ICOMT-K method achieved comparable or higher ARI with
only 3 or 4 clusters, indicating that it captured the main structure of the data
with fewer clusters. On the Customer Purchasing Behaviors dataset (Table 9),
the ICOMT-K method attained the highest ARI at depth 3, outperforming the
ICOT and PCT methods, which also have depth 3.
On the other hand, on the Real Estate Valuation dataset, the ICOMT-B
method achieved the highest ARI. Because this dataset has a relatively small
number of samples and exhibits many approximately linear relationships be-
tween the features and the target variable, discretization based on tercile splits
was sufficient to capture the data structure. In contrast, for data with high-
dimensional and nonlinear structures such as Statlog, using one-dimensional K-
means enabled distribution-aware discretization, which we believed contributed
to improved performance.
Focusing on differences across evaluation metrics, we observed that maximiz-
ing the Dunn index tended to increase the number of clusters for some datasets.
For example, on the Statlog dataset, the ICOMT-B method uses 2 clusters under
the setting that yielded the best silhouette coefficient, whereas it used 10 clusters
under the setting that yielded the best Dunn index. This suggests that when us-
ing the Dunn index, which prioritizes maintaining large inter-cluster distances,
finer partitions are more likely to be selected.


--- Page 20 ---
20
H. Suzuki et al.
Regarding computation time, compared with the ICOT and CART-Hybrid,
ICOMT-K and ICOMT-B methods required roughly comparable or slightly
longer computation time across all datasets. This is likely because, in addition to
solving the multiway-split tree structure exactly via 0–1 integer linear optimiza-
tion, the overall algorithm is more complex than the ICOT and CART-Hybrid
methods due to the inclusion of discretization procedures; however, for small-
scale datasets such as Seeds, Real Estate Valuation, and Customer Purchasing
Behaviors, the computation time was less than one second in all cases, which
is typically acceptable in practice. For a medium-scale dataset such as Statlog,
although the computation time of the ICOMT-K and ICOMT-B methods in-
creased compared with the ICOT and CART-Hybrid methods, solutions were
obtained within several tens of seconds to about 1–2 minutes, and we consider
this computational cost acceptable since the analysis is performed offline.
Overall, the ICOMT-K method shows stable performance across datasets,
indicating that adapting the discretization method to the data distribution con-
tributes to improved clustering accuracy. In addition, by adopting a multiway-
split tree structure, we were able to maintain high accuracy while keeping the
tree depth smaller than that of the ICOT method. We attribute this to the fact
that multiway branching can evaluate multiple features simultaneously, enabling
the model to capture the overall structure of the data with fewer splits.
3.4.2
Results on Interpretability Figures 7–11 show the decision-tree struc-
tures constructed by each method on the Statlog dataset under the setting that
yielded the best silhouette coefficient.
ICOMT-K Root
0.000 ≤ X12 < 0.248
0.248 ≤ X12 < 0.530
0.530 ≤ X12 ≤ 1.000
Cluster: 1
Number of data points: 482
Cluster: 2
Number of data points: 179
Cluster: 3
Number of data points: 184
Fig. 7. Decision tree constructed by ICOMT-K
In the ICOMT-K method shown in Fig. 7, the tree was shallow with depth
1, and the three clusters were clearly separated by a single feature (X12: scaled
variance along the minor axis). Each cluster was described by simple and intu-
itive conditions such as “0.000 ≤scaled variance along the minor axis < 0.248,”
indicating that the cluster boundaries were defined in a human-understandable
manner. Moreover, because the splitting rules concentrated on a single feature,


--- Page 21 ---
Interpretable Clustering via Optimal Multiway-split Decision Trees
21
ICOMT-B Root
0.362 ≤ X12 ≤ 1.000
0.000 ≤ X12 < 0.362
Cluster: 1
Number of data points: 282
Cluster: 2
Number of data points: 563
Fig. 8. Decision tree constructed by ICOMT-B
it was suggested that this feature may be a primary factor driving the separation
of the data.
The ICOT (Fig. 9) and PCT (Fig. 11) methods were based on binary-tree
structures, and because splits were performed sequentially, the trees tended to
become deeper, making it more difficult to grasp the overall structure. In con-
trast, the ICOMT-B and ICOMT-K methods adopted multiway-tree structures,
and because multiple branches could be represented simultaneously from a single
node, they could keep the tree depth small while maintaining sufficient expressive
power.
In particular, because the ICOMT-K method employed discretization via
one-dimensional K-means, the thresholds were adaptively determined based on
the data distribution, and we observed a tendency for within-cluster variability to
decrease. In contrast, in the ICOMT-B method, the split boundaries were defined
in a fixed manner by cumulative binning, resulting in somewhat coarser cluster
boundaries. Nevertheless, by adopting a multiway-tree structure, the ICOMT-B
method achieved interpretability comparable to or better than the ICOT method
with a shallower tree structure.
These results confirm that introducing a multiway-tree structure is effective
in simplifying the splitting rules while keeping the tree depth small. Therefore,
the ICOMT method can be regarded as a method that achieves both clustering
accuracy and interpretability.
4
Conclusion
4.1
Summary
In this papar, we extended the optimal multiway-split decision trees framework
to interpretable clustering and proposed a new method, ICOMT (Interpretable


--- Page 22 ---
22
H. Suzuki et al.
ICOT Root
X8 <= 0.271
X8 > 0.271
Cluster: 1
Number of data points: 267
X5 <= 0.753
X5 > 0.753
Cluster: 2
Number of data points: 574
Cluster: 3
Number of data points: 4
Fig. 9. Decision tree constructed by ICOT
CART-Hybrid
Root
X8 <= 0.271
X8 > 0.271
X10 <= 0.393
X10 > 0.393
X3 <= 0.722
X3 > 0.722
Cluster: 1
Number of
data points: 5
Cluster: 2
Number of
data points: 4
X2 <= 0.442
X2 > 0.442
Cluster: 3
Number of
data points: 1
Cluster: 4
Number of
data points: 257
X18 <= 0.517
X18 > 0.517
X18 <= 0.450
X18 > 0.450
Cluster: 5
Number of
data points: 263
Cluster: 6
Number of
data points: 52
X17 <= 0.350
X17 > 0.350
Cluster: 7
Number of
data points: 5
Cluster: 8
Number of
data points: 258
Fig. 10. Decision tree constructed by CART-Hybrid
Clustering via Optimal Multiway-split Decision Trees), that achieves both clus-
tering accuracy and interpretability.
The main features of the ICOMT method can be summarized as the following
three points.
1. Flexible split representation via a multiway-tree structure
We extend the binary-tree structure adopted in conventional ICOT to a
multiway-split tree structure, enabling combinations of features to be repre-
sented as more concise paths. As a result, we achieved high expressive power
while keeping the tree depth small, thereby improving the interpretability of
the overall model.
2. Adaptive discretization of continuous variables via one-dimensional
K-means
We introduced a method that applies one-dimensional K-means to each fea-
ture and set split boundaries based on the data distribution. This adaptive


--- Page 23 ---
Interpretable Clustering via Optimal Multiway-split Decision Trees
23
PCT Root
X12 <= 0.368
X12 > 0.368
Cluster: 1
Number of data points: 566
Cluster: 2
Number of data points: 279
Fig. 11. Decision tree constructed by PCT
discretization yielded boundaries suited to the cluster structure, contributing
to improved accuracy and more stable splitting rules.
3. Integrated determination of the global structure via an optimiza-
tion model
We formulated the multiway-split tree structure as a 0–1 integer linear opti-
mization problem and optimize cluster formation based on the tree structure
by obtaining an exact solution.
The numerical experiments showed that our ICOMT method achieved higher
clustering accuracy than existing methods, namely the ICOT, CART-Hybrid,
and PCT method, while producing simple splitting rules with a shallow tree
structure. In particular, the ICOMT-K method, which incorporated discretiza-
tion via one-dimensional K-means, outperformed the ICOMT-B method, which
used the conventional cumulative binning method, on many datasets.
Furthermore, the decision trees obtained by our method were shallow and
provided clear split conditions for each cluster, making it intuitive to under-
stand which features contributed to cluster formation. This property is useful
for visualizing clustering results and leveraging them in practical applications
such as decision-making and customer analysis.
4.2
Future Work
Although this paper demonstrated the effectiveness of our framework, the fol-
lowing issues remain for future development.
1. Extension to overlapping clustering
In the current ICOMT method, we assume an exclusive partition in which
each data point belongs to only one cluster. However, real-world data often
exhibit an “overlapping cluster” structure, where a data point can belong
to multiple groups of characteristics. An extension to a formulation that
allows partial cluster overlap while retaining the multiway-tree structure is
desirable.


--- Page 24 ---
24
H. Suzuki et al.
2. Automatic selection and evaluation of discretization methods
Although this paper adopts one-dimensional K-means, comparing it with
other discretization methods remains a topic for future work. In addition,
introducing an adaptive algorithm that selects different discretization meth-
ods for different features is also promising.
